<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> that is truly power which can only be wielded by the divine.<|5.82|>
<|7.62|> But offering up ourselves freely for another,<|10.52|>
<|11.46|> in sacrificial love, humility, and service,<|14.74|>
<|15.56|> that is within our power.<|18.36|>
<|18.36|> And that, I believe, is a uniquely human power.<|23.64|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It is the fullest expression of our humanity and the most perfect demonstration of human freedom.<|7.00|>
<|8.16|> It's a freedom to respond to God and one another in love that no one has the power to take from us.<|16.12|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The lens of secular history sees the crucifixion as a scandal and the end of Jesus' life.<|10.28|>
<|10.28|> The lens of faith sees the crucifixion as the glory of God and the doorway to new life.<|19.62|>
<|19.62|> Secular history tells us that Jesus is a victim of Roman cruelty and we should mourn his death.<|27.30|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The lens of faith sees a great high priest, one who freely offers himself to God on behalf<|7.78|>
<|7.78|> of humanity in the form of sacrificial love and whose life we celebrate.<|16.34|>
<|16.34|> Secular history sees weakness and powerlessness.<|19.70|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The lens of faith sees strength and power, which is proper both to God and power, which is proper to humanity.<|8.88|>
<|10.48|> The crucified Christ is not simply the one who died.<|13.94|>
<|15.00|> The crucified Christ shows us through his death who God is, what it means to be human, and how to live as a fully free human being.<|27.36|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Amen.<|1.54|><|endoftext|>
